Party_Details.Name: Kwee Lee Fung Ivon

Facts.Text: In 1988, Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd was incorporated and issued two shares, one each to Dr Lim and Dr Kwee.
At all material times, Dr Lim, Dr Kwee and Dr Lim’s mother, Mdm Irene Goh, were the company’s directors.
On 21 January 2010, Dr Kwee commenced divorce proceedings against Dr Lim.

In July 2010, Dr Lim incorporated another company, named 'Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd' ('the rival company'). He is the sole shareholder of the rival company, which, like the company, uses the Gleneagles property as its registered address.

From 1 October 2010 onwards, the rival company took over the company’s Gleneagles property as its place of business and paid the company an allegedly low monthly rental of $8,000 for the use of the said premises.
Dr Lim ceased to work under the company’s banner in order to helm the rival company’s medical practice.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, the operation of a new medical company by one equal shareholder-director at the same premises and the shift of business and rental arrangements created a conflict between that individual's personal business activities and the financial interests of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Under company law, a director who is involved in establishing or operating another business that uses the company’s premises or competes with the company is generally manage conflicts between personal interests and the company’s interests, including by making appropriate disclosures and ensuring that transactions with the company, such as rental arrangements, are on terms that are properly considered by the company.
Party_Details.Application: Dr Ivon Kwee Lee Fung and Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui each held half of the shares in Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd and were both directors, together with Mdm Irene Goh. The company operated a profitable medical practice at the Gleneagles property, with revenues exceeding one million Singapore dollars in 2008 and 2009 and 1,102,019 Singapore dollars in the first nine months of 2010. In July 2010, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui incorporated Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd, with himself as sole shareholder, using the same Gleneagles property as its registered address. From 2010-10-01, the new company took over the Gleneagles property as its place of business, paid Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd a monthly rental of 8,000 Singapore dollars, and Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui ceased working under Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s banner to lead the new company’s medical practice. After this change, Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s revenue for the last quarter of 2010 was 24,000 Singapore dollars. These events created a situation in which the new company’s operations and rental terms at the Gleneagles property were closely connected to the financial position of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge considered whether the statutory requirements under section 216A(3) of the Companies Act were satisfied, namely notice to directors, good faith of the complainant, and whether the proposed action appeared prima facie to be in the interests of the company. The judge treated it as the applicant’s burden to establish good faith and referred to appellate authority confirming this allocation. The judge accepted that hostility between the spouses existed but reasoned that hostility alone did not demonstrate bad faith, and examined whether the plaintiff was motivated by a vendetta or by an intention to damage or destroy the company. The judge concluded that the plaintiff’s financial interest in improving the company’s position and the value of her shares aligned with the company’s interest in recovering alleged lost profits, and that this alignment did not, by itself, indicate bad faith. The judge found that the defendant’s assertion that the application was brought solely to exert pressure was unsubstantiated. The judge distinguished Barrett v Duckett, noting that in that case the shareholder-plaintiff had effectively written off her interest in the company, opposed a winding up that was her only realistic route to value, and pursued litigation that was ruinous and had little prospect of recovery, whereas in the present case the plaintiff was pursuing a route that could enhance the value of her shares and the defendant appeared able to satisfy a judgment. The judge rejected the argument that the application was duplicitous in light of matrimonial proceedings, reasoning that the matrimonial court could not determine whether there had been a breach of directors’ or fiduciary duties and that denying leave could distort the picture of matrimonial assets by allowing the rival company’s profits to be treated solely as the defendant’s assets while the original company’s value was reduced. On the requirement that the action be prima facie in the interests of the company, the judge applied the approach that, at the leave stage, the court does not adjudicate on disputed facts or inferences and that the threshold is not high. The judge accepted that a doctor is generally free to set up or join another clinic but emphasised that a doctor-director practising through a company must still comply with directors’ and fiduciary obligations when doing so. The judge considered the plaintiff’s allegations that the defendant failed to act in the company’s interests, placed himself in a position of conflict, and did not disclose the potential conflict, and concluded that whether the defendant acted improperly was for the trial court to decide. The judge drew an analogy with Tam Tak Chuen v Eden Aesthetics Pte Ltd, where leave was granted in circumstances involving the transfer of a clinic’s business from companies jointly owned by two doctors to a new company controlled by one of them, resulting in a sharp drop in revenue for the original entities and substantial revenue for the new entity. In the present case, the judge noted that the defendant had set up a rival clinic at the same premises as the company’s clinic, that the company’s medical practice ceased and the company became a landlord collecting allegedly non-market rent from the rival clinic, and that these steps were said to have been taken without disclosure to the company’s board. The judge considered that these allegations of diversion of business and use of the company’s premises warranted a full hearing and that the proposed suit was legitimate and arguable. On the practical and commercial interests of the company, the judge reasoned that it would be in the company’s interests to seek to recover lost profits if the defendant had in fact breached his duties and that the defendant was in a position to pay damages. The judge rejected the suggestion that issues should be left entirely to the matrimonial ancillary proceedings. The judge concluded that the statutory threshold for granting leave under section 216A was met, granted the plaintiff leave to commence an action on behalf of the company against the defendant, ordered that the plaintiff have conduct of the proceedings because the defendant could not conduct proceedings against himself and the only other director was his mother who was also a director of the rival company, and ordered that the costs of the leave application and the company’s action be borne by the company on an indemnity basis.

Party_Details.Name: Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Issue: Whether the shift of medical practice operations from Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd to another company at the same premises, combined with the agreed rental level, affected the financial position and business model of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd in a way that justified a derivative action on its behalf.
Party_Details.Rule: For a derivative action under company law to proceed, it generally must appear that the company has an arguable claim arising from conduct that may have affected its business or financial position and that pursuing the claim is, on a preliminary view, in the company’s commercial interests.
Party_Details.Application: Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd operated a profitable medical practice at the Gleneagles property, with revenues exceeding one million Singapore dollars in 2008 and 2009 and 1,102,019 Singapore dollars in the first nine months of 2010. In July 2010, a new company, Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd, was incorporated with the same premises as its registered address. From 2010-10-01, the new company took over the Gleneagles property as its place of business, paid Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd a monthly rental of 8,000 Singapore dollars, and the medical practice previously conducted under Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s banner was conducted under the new company. Following this change, Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s revenue for the last quarter of 2010 was 24,000 Singapore dollars. These circumstances Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s business shifted from operating a medical practice to receiving rental income from the new company, with a significant change in revenue levels, forming the factual basis for considering a derivative action on its behalf.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge evaluated whether a proposed derivative action on behalf of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd met the statutory criteria. In assessing good faith, the judge focused on the plaintiff’s motivation and whether she sought to advance the company’s interests rather than pursue a personal vendetta. The judge concluded that the plaintiff’s objective of restoring or enhancing the company’s financial position and, consequently, the value of her shareholding, aligned with the company’s interests and did not indicate bad faith. The judge distinguished authority where litigation was pursued despite an insolvent or nearly insolvent company and where the plaintiff had effectively abandoned any real interest in the company. The judge also rejected the argument that the derivative action duplicated issues in matrimonial proceedings, reasoning that the matrimonial court could not determine whether there had been a breach of directors’ or fiduciary duties and that the absence of a derivative action could distort the assessment of matrimonial assets by inflating the value of the rival company relative to Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd. On whether the action was prima facie in the interests of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd, the judge applied the principle that, at the leave stage, the court does not resolve factual disputes or draw final inferences and that the threshold is relatively low. The judge considered that the company had operated a profitable clinic at the Gleneagles property, that a new company controlled by one of its directors had been set up at the same premises, that the new company took over the premises and paid rent to Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd, and that the original company’s revenue dropped sharply after the change. The judge regarded these circumstances as giving rise to a legitimate and arguable claim that warranted a full hearing. The judge further reasoned that it was in the practical and commercial interests of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd to pursue recovery of any lost profits if a breach of duty were ultimately established, particularly since the director involved appeared able to satisfy a judgment. The judge therefore granted leave for a derivative action to be brought in the name of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd, directed that the plaintiff have conduct of the proceedings because the director whose conduct was in issue could not manage litigation against himself and the only other director was closely connected to him and to the rival company, and ordered that the costs of the leave application and the company’s action be borne by the company on an indemnity basis.

Party_Details.Name: Gordon Lim Boon Hui

Facts.Text: Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui and Dr Ivon Kwee Lee Fung have five children, three of whom are graduates and two of whom are undergraduates.
In 1988, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui became a shareholder of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd with one issued share.
Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui was a director of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd.
At all material times, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui practised at Gordon Lim Clinic for Women operated by Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd at the Gleneagles property.
On 2010-01-21, divorce proceedings between Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui and Dr Ivon Kwee Lee Fung were commenced.
In July 2010, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui incorporated Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd, with himself as the sole shareholder, using the Gleneagles property as its registered address.

From 2010-10-01 onwards, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui stopped working under Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s banner and led the medical practice of Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd at the Gleneagles property.

From 1 October 2010 onwards, the rival company took over the company’s Gleneagles property as its place of business and paid the company an allegedly low monthly rental of $8,000 for the use of the said premises.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui’s establishment and operation of a new medical company at the same premises as Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd, and the associated rental and business arrangements, created a conflict between his personal business activities and the financial interests of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: A director who controls or participates in another business that uses the company’s premises or overlaps with the company’s business is generally manage any resulting conflict between personal interests and the company’s interests, including by making appropriate disclosures and ensuring that transactions with the company are considered and approved through proper corporate processes.
Party_Details.Application: Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui was a director and 50% shareholder of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd, which operated a profitable medical practice at the Gleneagles property. In July 2010, he incorporated Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd, with himself as sole shareholder, using the same Gleneagles property as its registered address. From 2010-10-01, the new company took over the Gleneagles property as its place of business, paid Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd a monthly rental of 8,000 Singapore dollars, and Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui ceased practising under Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s banner to lead the new company’s medical practice. After this change, Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd’s revenue for the last quarter of 2010 was 24,000 Singapore dollars, compared with higher revenues in 2008, 2009, and the first nine months of 2010. These circumstances Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui’s personal company operated from the same premises as Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd and that the financial outcomes for Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd were affected by the shift in business and rental arrangements.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge analysed Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui’s position as a director and equal shareholder of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd and his role in establishing and operating Gordon Lim Clinic and Surgery for Women Pte Ltd. The judge noted that, while a doctor is generally free to set up or join another clinic, a doctor who practises through a company and serves as its director must still comply with directors’ and fiduciary obligations when doing so. The judge considered the plaintiff’s allegations that Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui failed to act in the company’s interests, placed himself in a position of conflict, and did not disclose the potential conflict to the company’s board, but emphasised that whether he acted improperly was a matter for the trial court. At the leave stage, the judge applied the principle that the court does not adjudicate on disputed facts or draw final inferences and that the threshold for granting leave is not high. The judge drew support from Tam Tak Chuen v Eden Aesthetics Pte Ltd, where leave was granted in circumstances involving the transfer of a clinic’s business from jointly owned entities to a new company controlled by one doctor, resulting in a significant drop in revenue for the original entities and substantial revenue for the new company. In the present case, the judge highlighted that Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui had set up a rival clinic at the same premises as the company’s clinic, that the company’s medical practice ceased and the company became a landlord collecting allegedly non-market rent from the rival clinic, and that these steps were said to have been taken without disclosure to the company’s board. The judge regarded these allegations as sufficiently serious to merit a full hearing and concluded that the proposed suit against Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui was legitimate and arguable. The judge further reasoned that it would be in the practical and commercial interests of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd to seek to recover any lost profits if a breach of duty were ultimately established, particularly since Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui appeared able to satisfy a judgment. On this basis, the judge granted leave for an action to be brought on behalf of the company against Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui, directed that the plaintiff have conduct of the proceedings because Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui could not conduct litigation against himself and the only other director was his mother, who was also a director of the rival company, and ordered that the costs of the leave application and the company’s action be borne by the company on an indemnity basis.

Party_Details.Name: Mdm Irene Goh
Party_Details.Issue: Whether, before the dispute, Mdm Irene Goh’s position as a director of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd, alongside her son and the plaintiff, was the governance of decisions affecting the company’s business and premises.
Party_Details.Rule: Company law generally expects directors to participate in the governance of the company and to consider the company’s interests when decisions are made that affect its business operations and use of its assets.
Party_Details.Application: From 1988 onwards, Mdm Irene Goh served as a director of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd together with her son, Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui, and Dr Ivon Kwee Lee Fung. The company operated a medical practice at the Gleneagles property, and later the premises were used by a new company controlled by her son, with rental paid to Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd. Her role as a director placed her within the group responsible for overseeing the company’s affairs during the period when these changes to the company’s business and use of its premises occurred.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judge noted that Mdm Irene Goh was a director of Gordon Lim Clinic Pte Ltd and also a director of the rival company. In determining who should have conduct of the derivative proceedings, the judge reasoned that Dr Gordon Lim Boon Hui could not manage litigation brought against himself and that the only other director, Mdm Irene Goh, was closely connected to him and to the rival company. On this basis, the judge ordered that the plaintiff, rather than either of the other directors, have conduct of the proceedings. The judge also ordered that the costs of the leave application and the company’s action be borne by the company on an indemnity basis.
